=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969. * Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980. * History of Sakae Editorial Committee, 'History of Sakae,' Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 2013. * Yokohama City Buried Cultural Properties Center, '[https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/cms_files_maibun/pr_brochure/my031-hi.pdf Maibun Yokohama No. 31],' 2015. * Yokohama City Board of Education, '[https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.files/0007_20191120.pdf List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City],' 2019.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} ** {{ill|Miyanomae Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|宮ノ前横穴墓群}} ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|松ヶ崎横穴墓群}} * {{ill|Rock-cut tomb|ja|横穴墓}} * {{ill|List of burial mounds in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.html Cultural Properties and Buried Cultural Assets] - Official page of the Yokohama City Board of Education * [https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.files/0007_20191120.pdf List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City (PDF)] - Yokohama City Board of Education

=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:横穴墓]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:横穴墓]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969. * Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980. * History of Sakae Editorial Committee, 'History of Sakae,' Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 2013. * Yokohama City Buried Cultural Properties Center, '[https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/cms_files_maibun/pr_brochure/my031-hi.pdf Maibun Yokohama No. 31],' 2015. * Yokohama City Board of Education, '[https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.files/0007_20191120.pdf List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City],' 2019.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} ** {{ill|Miyanomae Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|宮ノ前横穴墓群}} ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|松ヶ崎横穴墓群}} * {{ill|Rock-cut tomb|ja|横穴墓}} * {{ill|List of burial mounds in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.html Cultural Properties and Buried Cultural Assets] - Official page of the Yokohama City Board of Education * [https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.files/0007_20191120.pdf List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City (PDF)] - Yokohama City Board of Education

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. '''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>

It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. revision.text:
'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969. * Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980. * History of Sakae Editorial Committee, 'History of Sakae,' Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 2013. * Yokohama City Buried Cultural Properties Center, '[https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/cms_files_maibun/pr_brochure/my031-hi.pdf Maibun Yokohama No. 31],' 2015. * Yokohama City Board of Education, '[https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.files/0007_20191120.pdf List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City],' 2019.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} ** {{ill|Miyanomae Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|宮ノ前横穴墓群}} ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|松ヶ崎横穴墓群}} * {{ill|Rock-cut tomb|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}} * {{ill|List of burial mounds in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.html Cultural Properties and Buried Cultural Assets] - Official page of the Yokohama City Board of Education * [https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.files/0007_20191120.pdf List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City (PDF)] - Yokohama City Board of Education

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Cave Tomb|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Cave Tomb Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site
== Related Articles == * [[Kofun period]] * [[Decorated kofun|Decorated Kofun]]
== References == * Nagahara, Keiji (1999). Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary. Iwanami Shoten. * Yokohama History Foundation (2013). History of Cave Tombs.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "鎌倉型横穴墓}}" to "鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}"
revision.text:
'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969. * Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980. * History of Sakae Editorial Committee, 'History of Sakae,' Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 2013. * Yokohama City Buried Cultural Properties Center, '[https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/cms_files_maibun/pr_brochure/my031-hi.pdf Maibun Yokohama No. 31],' 2015. * Yokohama City Board of Education, '[https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.files/0007_20191120.pdf List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City],' 2019.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} ** {{ill|Miyanomae Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|宮ノ前横穴墓群}} ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|松ヶ崎横穴墓群}} * {{ill|Rock-cut tomb|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}} * {{ill|List of burial mounds in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.html Cultural Properties and Buried Cultural Assets] - Official page of the Yokohama City Board of Education * [https://www.city.yokohama.lg.jp/kurashi/kyodo-manabi/bunkazai/bunya12000.files/0007_20191120.pdf List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City (PDF)] - Yokohama City Board of Education

=== Tokai Region === * {{ill|Kashiya Cave Tombs|ja|柏谷横穴群|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] ({{ill|Kannami|en}}[[ja:函南町]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 106 tombs * {{ill|Kitaema Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:北江間横穴群]] ({{ill|Izunokuni|en}}[[ja:伊豆の国市]], Shizuoka) - National Historic Site, 101 tombs * {{ill|E-no-ura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:江浦横穴群]] ({{ill|Numazu|en}}[[ja:沼津市]], Shizuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, 92 tombs
=== Hokuriku Region === * {{ill|Hōōzan Cave Tombs||ja|法皇山横穴古墳|en}}[[ja:法皇山横穴古墳]] ({{ill|Kaga, Ishikawa|en}}[[ja:加賀市]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 150 tombs
=== Kinki Region === * {{ill|Takaida Cave Tombs|en}}[[ja:高井田横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|en}}[[ja:柏原市]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Anfukuji Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:安福寺横穴群]] (Kashiwara, Osaka) - 32 tombs
=== Chūgoku Region === * {{ill|Jūōmen Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:十王免横穴群]] ({{ill|Matsue|en}}[[ja:松江市]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]) - 37 tombs * {{ill|Kitsunedani Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:狐谷横穴墓群]] (Matsue, Shimane) - 16 tombs * {{ill|Kamikonishi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:上塩冶横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Izumo, Shimane|en}}[[ja:出雲市]], Shimane) * {{ill|Takatsuku Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:高津久横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Chibu, Shimane|en}}[[ja:知夫村]], [[Oki District]])
=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Cave Tomb|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Cave Tomb Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.

Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.

revision.text:
'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Shichikokuyama tunnel tombs2.jpg|Current state of the tombs. The rear walls have been eroded due to land excavation. File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs. File:JReast Hongodaistation 20140319.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] in 2014. </gallery>

'''Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tomb Group''' (七石山横穴墓群) is a collection of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Kosugaya, Sakae Ward, Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is one of the subgroups of the larger {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}} and was registered as a {{ill|Yokohama City Registered Cultural Property|ja|横浜市登録地域文化財}} in 1988.<ref>Yokohama City Board of Education, 'List of Cultural Properties of Japan, Kanagawa Prefecture, and Yokohama City' (2019).</ref>
== Overview == The Shichikokuyama Tomb Group was constructed between the late [[7th century]] and the [[8th century]]. It originally consisted of more than 100 tombs around [[Hongōdai Station]], and over 200 tombs across 20 subgroups within the {{ill|Ōkawa River Basin Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群}}.<ref>Buried Cultural Properties Center (Yokohama City), 'Maibun Yokohama No. 31' (2015), pp. 1-2.</ref> However, due to urban development, only four tombs remain on the northern side of the railway tracks.
The tombs were discovered in [[1967]] during excavation work for the [[Negishi Line]]. The existence of the tomb group was reported by Kōnosuke Chigai, a member of the Yokohama City Local History Research Association. Excavations were conducted in [[1968]], [[1970]], and [[1972]].<ref>Yokohama City Cultural Property Research and Investigation Committee, 'Excavation Report on the Shichikokuyama and Takigakubo Yokouana Tomb Groups,' 1969.</ref><ref>Meisei University Archaeological Research Club, 'Hasekuboyama Site and Shichikokuyama Yokouana Tombs,' 1980.</ref>
The tombs are categorized as "{{ill|Kamakura-type rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鎌倉型横穴墓|1=Kamakura-style cave tombs}}" (also known as "{{ill|Kajigayatsu-style rock-cut tombs|lt=Cave tombs|ja|鍛冶ヶ谷式横穴墓}}"). These feature an arch-shaped cross-section, a well-developed [[burial chamber]] with a smaller "coffin chamber" at the back, and either a missing or significantly reduced entrance passage ([[dromos]]).<ref>Sakae Ward Regional Promotion Division, 'History of Sakae' (2013), pp. 20-21.</ref> Excavations uncovered human remains, [[hajiki]] and [[sue ware]] pottery, [[magatama]], [[tube-shaped beads]], [[iron swords]], [[iron knives]], and [[earrings]]. These findings suggest that the tombs were constructed continuously from the late [[Kofun period]] to the [[Nara period]], serving as burial sites for local elite rulers and wealthy farmers.

File:HongodaiSt.jpg|View of [[Hongōdai Station]] before apartment development (2006). The area was once a hilly landscape with many rock-cut tombs.
